sentences.text: This was the-- the-- the big hall behind the gas chambers.
It was very hot there, and it-- this was winter when we came.
And we-- and I said, gee, they make it so nice and hot, and the floors were clean, you know.
And the lights were on.
And everybody was so relaxed.
So first came a man-- two men.
And they said, OK.
Everybody gets undressed.
We got unclothed, and we go to the bath.
We had-- went to the gas chambers, but he couldn't-- they never tell this people, because they didn't-- they knew that we didn't know.
So they said, everybody get undressed.
We-- and everybody got a piece of soap.
Later I got the soap, we find that this was from children's fat.
Well, anyway, this is not what I want to tell you now.
So everybody gets undressed.
We got unclothed, and we going to get a shower because everybody was filthy and dirty, you know?
So everybody, you know, starts to take off the filth and the dirty clothes and everything.
And one looked at me and the other one looked at me.
I was probably the only one who was clean because I washed myself, you know?
And they came over to me, and just to me, they said, did you hear about Auschwitz?
I said, what Auschwitz?
They said, this is Auschwitz.
So I said, so what?
It's beautiful here.
I said, the lights, and it's so clean, and-- and we were such filthy camps.
Listen.
So they looked at-- I still didn't know nothing because I never heard of gas chambers.
And then he said to me, you never heard of gas chambers?
I said, no.
He said, it's a pity.
A pity that you haven't heard of gas-- like that.
I said-- he said, what are you doing here with the transport?
I said, I came voluntarily because my baby is here.
He says, sorry.
He said, volunteer, you came to death.
Like that, listen.
And I still didn't know nothing.
And people start to get undressed, and we had pieces-- I wasn't undressed yet, and mine child wasn't undressed yet.
And then I saw them standing and talking, talking.
And 10 minutes later, they came in with a Nazi SS guard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's when they made the selection.
So I impressed them, that he lets a few people out.
Listen.
So they said, you know, one here and one here.
One here, you know.
Selected people to-- and we didn't know what.
This is to life and this is to death, OK?
So they select me to life, and my baby goes with the other kids.
And I run over and I took mine baby.
I said, please don't take my baby.
I didn't know what it was all about it, but I didn't want to be separated from my child.
So-- and one of the men came over to me and said, shh.
Don't say nothing.
He said, you stay here.
She will stay here.
When he goes away, I will give you the baby.
Listen.
So sure enough, he selected like 12 women, 10 or 12 woman.
And a few men.
And then the Nazi left.
And there was in behind that-- there was a wall of windows.
And this was behind that where they put the people whom they select to life, you know.
A small room.
So they put us in there.
And then he took mine baby and brought it over to me through the window.
He got me mine Listen to that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I still didn't know, because that little room was so hot.
It was the wall from the gas chambers, for-- which-- was the crematorium.
The wall of the crematorium.
I still didn't know.
I had no idea.
And I was so happy that I had my baby.
And then came a-- a man, a German who was over prisoners.
I forgot his name.
I-- I remembered it.
Very tall.
About 7 foot tall.
Fat.
And he brought a piece of cake for my baby.
And he said his mother sends him this for the holiday, for the Christian holidays, for Christmas.
This was in January.
And he said, I want to give this to your child.
And he said, don't worry.
The children all will go to a-- to children's camp, he says, which is very close by where you will be.
I said, why can't I go to the children's camp?
I can wash floors.
I can keep-- watch the children.
I do some-- any work just to be next to my child.
He says, not-- you-- all the young people, the healthy one, have to go in the working camp.
But it will be only a couple of miles from you, he said.
So once a week, you can go in and see your child.
And if it's very hard on you, maybe we'll be able to do something that-- find you a job.
He didn't tell me that it goes to the gas chambers, you know.
And I still was sobbing with him.
He said, don't worry.
Your child will be safe, and you have to go to work, period.
And next morning, about 8 o'clock in the morning, I don't know, the-- the SS commandant came in.
And he says, OK.
You all have to go to unclothing, you know.
So I gave-- mine baby was sitting there waiting on the benches with other people, mine-- my pat, whatever I had, a little pat, a glass of something.
And she should have in the pack.
And I said, don't worry.
I am going to be soon back, because I thought we just going to-- to the unclothing.
I didn't know we'll come back and then we'll be separated.
And she started to cry.
And she says, Mommy, Mommy, please don't leave me.
I said, I'm not going to leave you.
Everybody's here.
We were only like 10 or 12 people, that's all.
So we were coming back, I said.
We're just going to-- to get some fresh clothes or something.
I don't know what he told me, that-- the guy there, the man.
I never saw my child again.
And they took us in-- in the camp in Birkenau in Auschwitz.
And they shaved our heads.
Shaved us all over, you know.
And then they put a number.
I was the 75th-- who came in the camp, you know.
That's the people who came alive.
Later on, they were killed anyway.
But I mean, that was my number.
And I asked one of the guards, after they did all this thing, I said, can I see my baby now?
So he says, look at the sky.
So I look, and I saw the big flames.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
And-- and-- and I said-- I said, what do you mean, your child?
And I said-- you see these big flames, he said?
And the big fire coming out?
That's where your child is.
That's where all of the children, all of them went.
I still didn't believe him.
I thought he's making me sick, you know?
Because this was the crematorium, right.
There were three or four crematoriums in Auschwitz.
And they were night and day going, and the skies were always black.
Stop for a minute.
I'm going to talk to her.
I will tell her.
Wait.
Wait.
--of what, I don't know.
Yeah.
And-- yeah.
When I went into-- when I-- they put a number on me.
And-- and the group of-- they put us in a-- in a block which was called-- I wrote it down.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I have it somewhere here.
Anyway, they put it first quarantine.
It's called quarantine block.
And from there, they select again people to the gas chambers.
And I still didn't know.
And from my group were only three-- three woman who came out alive.
Were only-- from this-- the rest from-- they took more people out to the gas chambers and more, you know, selected again.
And-- and one woman after the war went to Australia, her relatives, and one went to Israel.
But that's-- and one, I met that-- a-- the doctor who was came out with us, he was-- had a hunchback.
I met him at Bergen-Belsen after the liberation.
What-- that's all from the whole group what came out from-- from the selective group in Auschwitz, there were only-- nobody was alive, came out.
Yeah.
This was the block number 26 across from the crematorium.
I didn't know then about crematoriums in the beginning.
And it was-- we were like six or eight people.
I have-- have some pictures, no?
Laying in-- you know, there was three wooden-- wooden [POLISH], but I don't know how to say it in English, you know.
Well, you sleep like six people on-- and six and six, you know?
Three-- I-- we were in the middle, OK?
And we had every six-- 5 or 6 o'clock, we went on a-- we had to stay there to count us, Appell.
So the whistle blow, we have to get out and stay outside.
If it's snow or rain or cold, and they used to make us this-- this Nazi woman-- they were-- they were prostitutes who became the-- the Nazi woman-- on-- on woman, you know, in the camp.
They were wearing the big black capes.
One of them got fat like 300 pounds, 200 pounds.
They were the meanest bastard there is.
They made us jump in the swamps and carry big rocks, you know, all kinds of things.
And-- to suffer, you know.
Food was very, very little because the only food we used to get-- when used to get-- food is only for the transport who came-- went to-- straight to the gas chambers.
And people had some food with them in the pack.
And so they used to try give every little in the-- the barracks, you know.
Some food, you know.
And-- and after six weeks and-- yeah, and I-- they made me work in the [GERMAN], it was called, where you pick from the rocks, from the-- from the fallen train-- planes, you know?
There were a lot of-- because German was losing the war, the Germans, and-- but we didn't know that, but it's-- don't forget, 1944.
So they had all the shot down planes or helicopters where they were materials, all kind.
They made us make [GERMAN]-- how do you call it.
You know when they do-- make for the hair.
Braids?
Braid.
And long braids, as long as-- 10 different pieces of material, we used to make braids.
And they use this to clean the guns, to put in the guns and clean the guns.
And they did it because the-- this monster didn't want to go and fight the war.
They know they're going to lose.
So they made a factory of [GERMAN], of doing [GERMAN], it was called.
So they can be free and take care of us so they don't-- that they are useful to-- to the Nazi government, you see?
So that's what-- so we worked in the [GERMAN].
There were big group.

And I remember in the summer from 1944-- yeah, and every day, when they took us back to the-- to the block, to block 26, we had to cross the-- the-- yeah, and later they build it a straight railway to the gas chambers.
We had to go by the trucks.
Didn't have a railway.
But later on, we-- we-- we had to go-- the rock passing by the-- the crematorium, the gas chambers, where the [GERMAN] and this [GERMAN] was, you see?
So later on, they-- they-- we used to see them working on the railways, and they made the railways, you know.
And one thing I don't forget, it was September, 1944.
Close to the Jewish holidays.
I don't-- that-- in the fall, anyway.
And we were the group, you know, working from the [GERMAN], going back to the barrack.
And we had to stop because a transport came with a lot of children and people going to the gas chambers.
We couldn't cross the railway because we had to cross, and there was a long, long transport.
And we had to stay and wait until they all went into the gas chambers.
And I looked at the faces of the kids from the little windows like I was staying with mine child and looking through.
And I said, there's no God.
I'm sorry.
If there's a God and he can let those little-- those people go on like that to the gas chambers, those innocent children-- I gave up God.
And the point is that they were asking for water.
And I knew how they feel-- we had no water.
We couldn't move.
We couldn't even make one step, because we were standing with the guns and watching us.
So that's when I said, that's it.
I-- I don't believe in God anymore.
Because when I saw these children and the people going to the gas chambers.
We had to wait for an hour.
That's all what it took them.
Took them in, and that's it.
Sure.
I-- you had it in my paper.
January-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the end of January, I think.
I don't know.
In-- they liquidated the camps in Estonia.
Oh, God in heaven.
We were for a whole year, almost.
Over a year.
For about a year, we were.
Because I was in the last transport from Auschwitz-Birkenau which was liquidated.
I'm-- because we working.
We were working in the [GERMAN], rocks.
And the feet, I didn't have any shoes.
And we met a group of men from another camp coming by us.
And they said in Polish to us, girls, if you can hide, hide.
Because the Russians-- the war is almost over.
The-- the-- the Russians are not far from us.
And the same night when we were-- Auschwitz was liberated, but we were there.
Till January.
Till--
Right.
27 January of '45.
This, I remember.
That's when we left.
And that's when-- when Birkenau Auschwitz was liberated, the night.
Well, they-- there was selections constantly.
But--
I saw people who selected and taken out from my barrack, but they didn't take me.
I don't think so.
I don't-- I can't remember.
I want to think, and I can't.
I know that I-- I was sick, and they wanted to put me in the barrack, and I know if you go in the barrack, you go to the gas chambers.
So I didn't go.
I was sick, and I stayed out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Can't remember it at-- at the moment anything.
They're talking.
It's loud.
This was in the [GERMAN].
We were only in the [GERMAN].
We-- that's what we were.
We were-- we-- they-- oh, yes.
In this November, December, we had a lot of air raids-- bombs falling-- which was the happiest time of my life then.
Because when we had the-- the-- the bombs, the-- the air raids, you would see the Nazis like little rats.
They were hiding.
We were watching them through the window because-- we-- we used to see when we were sitting, and we were so happy.
But we saw the Nazis-- our big leaders, you know-- hiding, running in the-- in the shelters from the bombs.
They were hiding.
We didn't have where to hide.
We were-- we didn't care.
We waited for a bomb, you know.
So-- but this-- this-- the bombing they did was around Auschwitz and Birkenau, around-- not in the camp, because they knew that there were people, innocent prisoners.
Innocent slaves.
So they didn't bomb the camp itself, but they bombed around the camp.
And all the Nazis were hiding.
You would see how they were running when we were to the windows, it's-- not till we live.
And one more thing.

In November or December-- I think this was November-- a-- a-- the-- you know the-- the Sonderkommandos, when the people who used to burn and gas the people, every three month, they took them and they burn-- they gassed them and-- they took them on trains, they gassed them.
And every three months, they collect new ones.
In fact, one of the girls who were with me in the camp, her brother who came from Estonia was taken over to be a Sonderkommando.
A young boy of 19 years or something was-- he was a big, tall young man.
So they took them all on the-- in the trains, and they put the gas in and they gassed them.
But this last Sonderkommando decided, I'm not going to be gassed.
They're going to fight for it.
So we remember like today there-- when they wanted to put him in the-- the-- all the big-- the big Nazis put them in the trains, they revolted.
They find grenades they stole from the-- knives, anything.
And they were fighting.
They were all killed, but they were killed like men.
They weren't taken like sheep to the gas chambers.
So that was the revolt from the Sonderkommando, the last one.
I think it was October or November.
I don't remember.
And after that, they-- they closed-- the-- one of the-- the crematoriums, they-- they closed.
And they moved us to another camp, Lager [GERMAN].
C.
Lager C.
And they liquidated-- Lager B was in Birkenau, and the other camps.
Because they knew that the front are close by.
So they-- so we were taken to Lager C.
And this was even worse, because we didn't have even a piece of bread.
There was no chance of a piece-- of water, you know.
And we were Lager C until the-- you know-- and the gypsies-- there was a camp for the gypsies where they took one day and they put them all in the gas chambers.
We were walking to [GERMAN], and they were-- and this were the same-- and they were going, the gypsies, with their-- they had the gypsies with their families and their children.
And they were all-- whole thing.
And then we find out that the whole-- the whole gypsy camp was completely annihilated with the children, with the mothers and fathers.
They were the only-- and I remember there were only some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] religious Christian, you know-- I don't remember.
They were-- [INAUDIBLE], I don't remember, which were liquidated.
But then they started to liquidate Auschwitz by December.
December-- by January, slowly, because they knew that the Russians are already there.
So the-- and we were the only group which was left, was the-- my group.
So in January 27, we were the last people from Auschwitz Birkenau who came out.
After us, we-- they closed it, you see?
And we had no clothing.
I told you, we-- we walked for five days and five nights.
And for five days and five nights, we were in the-- the coal wagons.
You know the little wagons when [GERMAN] for the-- they were the coal mines, you know?
So we were all black.
I remember the-- the SS, the Nazi looked at that.
And one of them said, well, you look so black like gypsies.
And then he said-- one of them, I remember when we-- when we came to-- when we came to Bergen-Belsen-- no, wait.
Before.
This was the fifth night when we were walking, we couldn't walk anymore, you know?
So I couldn't walk.
I just fall.
It was in the snow.
We walked on side roads over.
So one of the Nazis, an-- an old German soldier came over.
And he said to me, get up.
Get up.
Walk, he said.
The-- the war is almost over for you.
I said, I can't walk.
He said, you better try.
Because you know what the young one, the young Nazis, everybody who fall-- because a lot of people fall the side and they couldn't walk, you know?
So they used to go and they shoot them and shoot them.
Took off their heads, stomachs, the-- you know, they-- so that old man came over by.
And I was laying there.
And he said to me, you better start walking.
The war is over for you.
I said, I can't.
He said, try.
So I don't know how I tried with my last strength, and then we went into a-- a barn there somewhere.
And we stayed, you know.
Where-- there was no place.
And we were standing, you know, one on-- next to the other, like that.
And then in the morning, finally they got the coal wagons, you know.
And they put us in and took us another five days and five nights, and we came to-- to Bergen-Belsen.
But you know, the-- the nights when we were walking and when I fall-- every night I used to fall by the time we reach where they stopped.
They walked over my-- my-- my body.
Did fall, and-- and didn't hurt.
I was laying like that, and everybody with the feet and with the hand and with-- was walking over my body.
And I came out.
It's incredible.
And if you have to survive, you survive no matter what.
But I gave--
--up, as I say.
I want to make a-- a point.
I'm alone, you know, and sick.
And many times, I try-- I speak to God Almighty, wherever he is.
And I said, listen.
How much of this-- how much do you have to suffer?
I suffered enough.
And-- but in-- in-- inside, in my heart, I will never forgive-- if there is a God or Jesus Christ or whoever there, that they let this happen, you know?
The gas chambers.
Never forgive, and let innocent children.
Grown up, they say-- they seen-- they do wrong things.
But children?
Children don't know what's wrong, what to do wrong.
OK.
That was just my statement, that I don't believe in any-- anything anymore.
Only when I'm alone.
[LAUGHS] Can you tell me about the conditions that you found in Bergen-Belsen when you arrived?
Oh, well.
Bergen-Belsen was the end of the-- the game.
There was no water.
There was no food because the war was-- was around Bergen-Belsen, OK?
The British, this-- were fighting the Nazis.
And there was no supply of any food.
I remember only once there came a plain wagon, you know, pulled by a horse, with some bread for the Christian-- there were Christian prisoners too, you know?
And there were-- there were Russian-- White-- White Belarus-- White-- White Russians woman there, with the-- you know?
And they attack that little wagon with the bread and they grabbed all the bread.
But we never had not a sign of bread or any food.
We were from a-- when we came, in February?
We were 2 and 1/2 months before the liberation, we were in-- in Bergen-Belsen.
So April 15-- March, February-- so we came February by the time we got to Bergen-Belsen.
And we have-- the conditions was terrible.
No more water.
We were laying on the-- on the-- on the ground, and the same-- the same-- take away the-- the-- there was not-- it was not wooden building.
There was-- you know, the same condition.
And people were dying like flies in the barrack where I was.
You know, we were laying on the floor.
When we came in the barrack were 15,000 people in that barrack.
Woman.
And you know after the liberation, there weren't even 40.
Everybody-- I was crawling too, you know.
I can't stand up.
Because there was no food.
And diseases.
I survived for one reason, because I had typhus and dysentery in Estonia, you see.
So my body was immune.
I couldn't get it again.
So when everybody was dying around me, I was alive.
And when the British liberated us-- this will never forget.
Big soldiers.
Big men-- no, wait a moment.
They were standing up like statues, and they were like frozen.
They couldn't believe it, their eyes, because there were mountains of dead.
You see, the crematorium used to burn the dead bodies wasn't working anymore.
And there was big, big mountains of dead bodies laying, you know.
One, two, three, four.
The only thing what I saw is dead bodies and people crawling around on all four like little mice, you know?
So they were flabbergasted.
They were-- they couldn't see it move.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Yes.
Who is it?
Grass.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- you see, the-- the Nazis had a kitchen where they used to cook for the people there, for the Nazis, for-- so we used to find sleeves of potatoes peels, you know.
It-- it was no-- no food.
That's why everybody was dying, from diseases and no food. 16,000 people died after the liberation, or 17,000, in Bergen-Belsen.
They know that.
They have-- everything is taken.
Very good.
Like-- they treated us-- treated me like a little child.
They burned down-- they-- after seven-- six-- because it was all the-- the diseases, you know?
So they burned down Bergen-Belsen.
They wipe even it out with the ground.
And they moved out to Bergen-Belsen, which is where the German Nazis stay, where the-- the soldiers were in, like regular barracks.
That's where they moved the people.
And-- and Belsen was completely liquidated.
Burned down to the ground.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She's doing this on purpose, you know?
That woman.
Did you try after the war to go home at all, to-- to look for family members?
To home?
Well, I know everybody was dead.
Hohne.
The original camp Bergen-Belsen was wiped out.
We stayed in part of Bergen-Belsen where the German soldiers and military, [GERMAN] house.
And that time, I worked for UNRRA.
Because-- yeah.
Well, the British opened an office, and-- and I became one of the-- I worked for the-- for the vice director and director from UNRRA.
Mr.
Kidman ?] and Mr.
Bloomberg.
You have the-- the names.
I can't remember.
And because of that, I used to get extra ration of food, because we had no food.
But they used to give us-- for people who worked, we used to get a piece of bread and some milk, you know.
Something through the British government.
Because the Germans also didn't have at that time food, any food there.
And-- and I was-- they treated me very well.
They treat me like their child, the-- the-- I have nothing to complain about.
Well, I used to do translation from German to English, from Polish, from Yiddish, to English, and vice versa, you know.
Because they needed all the information what they could.
And that's what I did.
So I can get up now?
Because I'm so hot.
I'm sweating.
